title: Interactives
text: WebAssembly (Wasm) is a binary instruction format for a stack-based virtual machine. It is designed to be a portable target for the compilation of high-level languages like C, C++, and Rust, enabling them to run on the web at near-native speed.

title: Interactives
text:
Pyodide for Python
Pyodide is a project that brings the Python runtime to the browser using WebAssembly. It allows you to run Python code in the browser, making it possible to create interactive Python applications without needing a server.

title: Interactives
text:
WebR for R
WebR is a similar project that brings the R programming language to the browser using WebAssembly. It allows you to run R code in the browser, enabling interactive R applications without the need for a server.

title: Quarto Live
text:
Packages
For example, to load packages, you can include the following in the YAML header:

RPython


---
format: live-html
webr:
  packages:
    - ggplot2
    - dplyr
---


---
format: live-html
pyodide:
  packages:
    - matplotlib
    - numpy
    - seaborn
---



Check out the Loading Packages Documentation for more information, including how to load from custom repositories.






WarningUsing both R and WebR code blocks



If you are combining R code blocks with WebR code blocks (or Python with pyodide), you will need to load packages in both places, as they run in separate environments. The R code blocks will not have access to packages loaded in WebR code blocks, and vice versa (similar for Python).








WarningPackage availability in WebR



Make sure to check if an existing package is available in WebR before trying to load it, as not all R packages are currently supported. You can check the list of available packages. If this link takes too long to load, you can also use this searchable repository created by Bob Rudis.

title: Quarto Live
text:
Files and data
From Quarto’s website:

Normally webr and pyodide code blocks are executed in the reader’s web browser, and so resources local to your own machine are not available for use inside the WebAssembly environment. Instead, there is a Virtual Filesystem (VFS) made available that contains only the minimum required to run R or Python code.

To make additional resources available, you can use the resources option in the YAML header to specify files to be loaded into the VFS. This can include data files, images, or any other resources that your code blocks need to access. For example, if you have a data directory in your Quarto project with multiple datasets, you can include the following in the YAML header to make the entire directory available in the VFS:
---
format: live-html
resources:
  - data
---
Then, you can access the files in the data directory from your code blocks using the path data/filename. For example, if you have a file called mydata.csv in the data directory, you can read it into a WebR code block like this:

RPython


```{webr}
mydata <- read.csv("data/mydata.csv")
```


```{pyodide}
import pandas as pd
mydata = pd.read_csv("data/mydata.csv")
```



You can find more information about this feature in the Loading Resources Documentation






WarningUsing both R and WebR code blocks



Just like with loading packages, if you are combining R code blocks with WebR code blocks, you will need to load data in both places, as they run in separate environments. The R code blocks will not have access to data loaded in WebR code blocks, and vice versa.

title: Quarto Live
text:

title: Quarto Live
text:
Prettifying interactive exercises






TipDefault aesthetics



Plot a rectangle using geom_tile centered at (x,y) = (4, 7). Do not specify any other aesthetics.















NoteHint 1





1ggplot() +
  geom_tile(___)

1

Add the geom_tile geom and specify the aesthetics by using the aes() function and assigning it to the mapping argument of geom_tile.













NoteHint 2





1ggplot() +
2  geom_tile(mapping = aes(___))

1

Add the geom_tile geom and specify the aesthetics by using the aes() function and assigning it to the mapping argument of geom_tile.

2

Specify the x and y aesthetics as arguments to the aes() function, assigning them their appropriate values.













TipSolution







ggplot() + #<1>
  geom_tile(mapping = aes(x = 4, y = 7)) #<2>
1ggplot() +
2  geom_tile(mapping = aes(x = 4, y = 7))



1

Add the geom_tile geom and specify the aesthetics by using the aes() function and assigning it to the mapping argument of geom_tile.

2

Specify the x and y aesthetics as arguments to the aes() function, assigning them their appropriate values.















Markdown

::: {.callout-tip title="Default aesthetics"}
*Plot a rectangle using* `geom_tile` *centered at (x,y) = (4, 7). Do not specify any other aesthetics.*


```{webr}
#| exercise: one-tile-ex-1
#| autorun: FALSE

```

::: { .hint exercise="one-tile-ex-1"}
::: { .callout-note collapse="false"}
## Hint 1

```{r}
ggplot() +       #<1>
  geom_tile(___) #<2>
```
1. Add the `geom_tile` geom and specify the aesthetics by using the `aes()` function and assigning it to the `mapping` argument of `geom_tile`.

:::
:::

::: { .hint exercise="one-tile-ex-1"}
::: { .callout-note collapse="false"}
## Hint 2

```{r}
ggplot() + #<1>
  geom_tile(mapping = aes(___)) #<2>
```
1. Add the `geom_tile` geom and specify the aesthetics by using the `aes()` function and assigning it to the `mapping` argument of `geom_tile`.
2. Specify the x and y aesthetics as arguments to the `aes()` function, assigning them their appropriate values.
:::
:::

::: { .solution exercise="one-tile-ex-1"}
::: { .callout-tip collapse="false"}

## Solution

```{webr}
#| exercise: one-tile-ex-1
#| solution: TRUE
ggplot() + #<1>
  geom_tile(mapping = aes(x = 4, y = 7)) #<2>
```
1. Add the `geom_tile` geom and specify the aesthetics by using the `aes()` function and assigning it to the `mapping` argument of `geom_tile`.
2. Specify the x and y aesthetics as arguments to the `aes()` function, assigning them their appropriate values.

```{webr}
#| exercise: one-tile-ex-1
#| check: TRUE
gradethis::grade_this_code()
```

:::
:::
:::

title: Authoring
text:
When rendering to HTML documents, you can include links to other formats in the YAML header. This allows you to create multiple versions of the same document, each with a different output format. For example, you could create an HTML version for web viewing and a PDF version for printing. You can find more information about this feature in the Quarto documentation.



HTML Accessibility Checks

title: Authoring with Code
text:
RStudio has a built-in connection to GitHub’s Copilot. Go to Tools > Global Options > Code > Copilot to enable it.

title: Useful Extensions
text:
HTML

HTML


Name
Description
Example




bluesky-comments
Quarto Shortcode Extension to Embed Comments from Bluesky and add Bluesky-powered comment sections for websites and HTML outputs
Link


elevator
Shortcode for using Elevator.js in HTML documents
Link


embedio
Variety of shortcodes for including files and content in HTML documents



lightbox
Create lightbox treatments for images in your HTML documents (see also the built-in functionality)



modal
A Quarto extension providing a simple way to create Bootstrap modals in your HTML documents



nutshell
Embed Nutshell expandable explanations in HTML documents



remember
An extension for Quarto to save and restore navigation position across page visits for both HTML documents and Reveal.js presentations



social-embeds
Embed content from across the web into HTML documents using a shortcode



social-share
Add buttons to share articles on various social media platforms






Coding

Coding


Name
Description




code-appendix
A lua filter to auto-generate an appendix from code chunks


output-styling
Style computational output, errors, warnings, and messages in HTML documents


pyodide
Embed Pyodide in HTML documents to enable interactive Python code cells without the need for a Python server


quarto-live
WebAssembly powered ‘live’ formats for Quarto HTML documents and Revealjs slides, adding interactive code blocks and exercises for R and Python


qreacto
Run standalone React components in your project


regurgitate
Automatic Code Appendix containing all shown code with an option to include results or group by language


shinylive
This extension lets you embed Shinylive applications in a Quarto document.


webr
Embed WebR in HTML documents to enable interactive R code cells without the need for an R server





Teaching

Teaching


Name
Description




assign
A Quarto Extension for educators writing assignments by combining instruction and solution authoring (Note: Some if this functionality is built-in to Quarto through Conditional Content)


honeypot
Add hidden instructions to HTML homework assignments to help detect cheating by unauthorized LLM usage


quizdown
Add an interactive quiz into your HTML document





General Utility

General Utility


Name
Description




linkate
Automatically collect all URLs in a document and place them at the end under a “Links” section


molstar
Shortcode to embed proteins and trajectories with Mol*


open-social-comments
Enables adding Bluesky or Mastodon/Fediverse powered comments to posts and articles


unsplash
Shortcode to use Unsplash images within documents





Math/LaTeX

Math/LaTeX


Name
Description




bibentry
A Quarto extension that replicates LaTeX’s \bibentry command functionality. It allows you to display full bibliography entries inline within your document text


callouty-theorem
Wraps theorems and proofs in callout blocks for better visual appeal


custom-amsthm-environments
Quarto extension for defining custom amsthm environments


imagify
Convert embedded LaTeX to images and use .tex/.tikz files as image sources


latex-environment
Output divs as custom LaTeX environments


tikz
A filter that renders PGF/TikZ diagrams in HTML as SVG


typst-math
Changes math block syntax from LaTeX to Typst math
